claim.source_sentence: - Actions (in Section 3.1) are critical, but not defined clearly. I have no problems with actions for construction heuristics, but actions for improvement heuristics are not well defined. In Section 3.2 “Insertion Position Embedding” (P5), the definition of insertion position is undefined clearly, and why the number of choices is (n+m) for each operation. Besides, it is also unclear about why the total number of insertion positions is equal to n×(n+m). For these unclear descriptions, there is no clue to understand the proposed method. Note that in Section 3.2 “Policy Model” (P6), there is no way to understand the description “Obviously, there are at most m different insertion schemes for each improvement decision.”
claim.text: Actions (in Section 3.1) are critical, but not defined clearly. I have no problems with actions for construction heuristics, but actions for improvement heuristics are not well defined.

However, the meta-review adds additional critiques beyond the exact claim, but the core concern about unclear definitions is present and relied upon. This makes the concern survive, though not exhaustively focused on the exact wording, hence partial rather than survived. The high quality of the concern is indicated by specific mentions in the source field and matched sentences.

claim.source_sentence: This paper, TVTSv2, is the second version of TVTS paper. It focuses on the foundation model of video representation learning. Specifically, this paper first points out the so called degradation issue existing in video representation field. Based on this degradation observation, this paper proposes a hypothesis that such degradation is from the noisy text data. Accordingly, it freezes the shallow layers of text encoder while training the deeper layers to alleviate this issue. In this way, the zero-shot performance is significantly improved to show the great generalization ability of the proposed training strategy.
claim.text: The paper focuses on the foundation model of video representation learning and claims zero-shot performance is significantly improved by freezing shallow layers and training deeper layers.

meta_review.matched_segment: The authors claim that this model realizes task-agnostic video representation learning.

rationale: The meta-review reiterates and endorses the key concern from the review—that freezing the text encoder to improve zero-shot performance relates to degradation from end-to-end fine-tuning. It treats this as a substantive finding and cites it in the analysis of the paper's contribution and its reception. Therefore the concern survives and is relied upon by the AC. The issue is stated with clear justification and is actionable within the paper's discussion of methodology and its impact on task-agnostic representation, making it high-quality. The item should be excluded from training data only if ambiguity existed; here evidence is clear and explicit.
title: TVTSv2: Learning Out-of-the-box Spatiotemporal Visual Representations at Scale

claim.source_sentence: While I understand the rationale and setup of the problem for translating motion vector inputs as coresets and tracking, the results on the two datasets are not convincing. While the paper talks a lot about how this approach is substantially better in comparison to neural-net based methods, it fails to refer to any of the classic methods in tracking where clustering, robust statistical methods are used. The paper does refer to a review paper and states that there are over 1000 articles on the subject. However, if the central aim of the paper is to demonstrate the advancement in tracking algorithms the paper should demonstrate the effectiveness of the algorithm designed by comparing it with at least one alternative (e.g. mean-shift based tracking , Comaniciu et al (CVPR 2000)). I note that the mean-shift based tracker performed in real-time in low computational power settings for given candidate regions in a video over two decades ago.

claim.source_sentence: The authors claim that current QA-based medical evaluation datasets cover only some common diseases. However, in section 3.1 where the authors introduce their proposed knowledge base, it is said that "We first select a subset from the ICD10 database according to whether the diseases are common in clinical (determined by clinical experts) and are statistically frequent in EHR (Electronic Health Record), resulting in 10,632 common diseases." I wonder why they also consider common diseases in their knowledge base?
claim.text: Clarify why common diseases are considered in the knowledge base since ICD10-based selection cites common diseases for MedDisK.

meta_review.matched_segment: The authors assert, but without much argument, that LLMs must "master adequate knowledge" to be used in this space, but this is not clear to me a priori.

rationale: The meta-review acknowledges concerns about motivation and validity of the evaluation and notes calls for better framing and evidence for the measure; it does not fully endorse the claim that models master adequate clinical knowledge, but discusses it as a point of critique. This maps to a partial survival: the concern is present but not fully endorsed, with high-quality, explicit discussion to justify evaluation validity. The meta-review neutrally mentions and analyzes the concern without fully relying on it as a proven claim.
title: Do Current Large Language Models Master Adequate Clinical Knowledge?

claim.source_sentence: 1. I like the general idea of the proposed evaluation framework, but my biggest concern about this framework is the heavy use of DistilBERT accuracies in the evaluation framework. For the faithfulness metric, the framework is evaluating the performance of DistilBERT on the generated dataset. This confounds faithfulness with the difficulty (or complexity) of the dataset. This makes some of the finding questionable. For example, is there really a tradeoff between faithfulness and diversity/complexity, or is this correlation comes from the correlation between difficulty and diversity/complexity? I wonder if the authors can provide gold evaluation results for the DistilBERT models.
claim.text: The heavy use of DistilBERT accuracies in the evaluation framework confounds faithfulness with the difficulty (or complexity) of the dataset, making some findings questionable.

meta_review.matched_segment: This paper discusses multiple automatic metrics to evaluate synthetic data generated by LLMs, considering different models and training regimes, namely: faithfulness, diversity, conformity, complexity, and performance.

rationale: The reviewer concern about heavy use of DistilBERT accuracies confounding faithfulness with dataset difficulty is echoed in the meta-review as part of weaknesses: mentions issues with metric definitions and lack of empirical evidence. However, the meta-review text discusses broader evaluation metrics and does not explicitly endorse the specific DistilBERT confounding claim as a core finding, and the connection is not deeply substantiated in the meta-review. The overlap is semantic but the meta-review only neutrally mentions related concerns; thus survival is partial. The quality is high because it's a precise, actionable critique about metrics and empirical support.
title: Understanding Large Language Models Through the Lens of Dataset Generation

Therefore survival is high, and the concern quality is high, with clear evidence. Training use is included because it's reliable for evaluation/training.

claim.source_sentence: This paper proposes an end-to-end RL framework to solve the Flexible Job-Shop Problem (FJSP). The framework consists of two major components: a generation model that produces an assignment of operations that updates the partial solution, and an improving model that refines the current partial solution. By repeating the generation and improving steps until the complete solution is found, the proposed framework finds a solution for FJSP.
claim.text: The proposed framework suggests a novel perspective for solving FJSP. Unlike the majority of iterative improving approaches that often perform improvement steps from a complete solution, the proposed framework employs "improving" actions during solution construction.

meta_review.matched_segment: Strengths: The paper's strength lies in its novel dual-model approach to solving FJSP, which combines solution generation and improvement in a concurrent training framework.

rationale: The meta-review explicitly summarizes the paper’s two-model framework for simultaneous generation and improvement in FJSP, and states strengths, notably the novelty of the dual-model approach and claims about speed and quality. It endorses the idea (strengths section) and relies on the same concern (dual-model concurrent generation and improvement). The concern is specific (dual-model RL framework for FJSP), well-supported by the meta-review text, and actionable for evaluation. The match is not merely lexical but semantic.
title: SIMULTANEOUS GENERATION AND IMPROVEMENT: A UNIFIED RL PARADIGM FOR FJSP OPTIMIZATION

claim.source_sentence: (2) Conclusions are overstated. The paper states "we will see that discarding the old metaphor may question the interpretation of recent studies aiming at characterizing the values, personality traits, social skills or moral values of LLMs using tools developed to measure attributes of human psychology". The current status of the argument has not led to this conclusion directly. The paper needs to reconnect and build out a cohesive careful argument in order to support this claim.

claim.source_sentence:

- Finally, provide a detailed discussion on why various algorithms belonging to each category succeed or fail on various tasks with various difficulty levels.
claim.text: The authors suggest categorising the aspects that make the benchmark challenging and evaluating multiple state-of-the-art algorithms per category across difficulties; this is a suggested change or experiments.

The claim text is about a suggested change in experimental design; the meta-review asks for clarity and mentions concerns, but does not substantively repeat or endorse that exact concern. Therefore semantic survival is not_found. claim.source_sentence: The first paragraph is problematic. “implicitly assume that node representations learnt by GNNs are independent conditioned on node features and edges, thereby ignoring the joint dependency among nodes...” does not represent the related works accurately. These works do not ignore dependency among the nodes, which is captured via multiple rounds of message passing similar to loopy belief propagation. I find the first paragraph could be phrased in a different way to make the distinctions accurate.

claim.source_sentence: - The proposed work's claimed distinction with CaP is unclear. But, the proposed methodology also uses LLMs to generate Python programs, so the distinction is unclear.
